name: Comb Ridge
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding along Utah's dramatic monocline ridge featuring colorful agate, jasper, and petrified wood. Free collecting with no permit required.
description:
Comb Ridge is a towering monocline formation in San Juan County that stretches over 80 miles and offers excellent rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land. The ridge's exposed geological layers make it ideal for finding colorful agates, jasper specimens, and fragments of petrified wood weathering out from sandstone and shale formations. The area is relatively undiscovered compared to other Utah rockhounding destinations, making it a great choice for collectors seeking solitude and uncrowded digging.

The geology here reflects the Cretaceous and Tertiary layering typical of the Colorado Plateau. Mineral-rich fluids percolating through sandstone layers created the agate and chalcedony deposits, while ancient forests preserved as petrified wood can be found along erosional washes. The colorful banding in many specimens reflects iron oxide variations and silica precipitation over millions of years.

Most productive collecting occurs along the base of the ridge and in shallow washes where weathering has exposed specimens. The terrain is relatively open pinyon-juniper scrubland with moderate elevation gain in places. Water is scarce, so bring plenty. The ridge offers stunning panoramic views and excellent photography opportunities alongside your rockhounding.

Collecting here is straightforward - no permit required, personal use only, and you can take up to 25 lbs per day. The location works well for families and beginners willing to hike moderate distances. Early morning collecting followed by ridge exploration makes for a full rockhounding day.
history: Comb Ridge formed during the Laramide Orogeny as a monocline - a steep fold in originally flat-lying rock layers. Cretaceous sandstones and shales were pushed upward, creating ideal conditions for silica-rich groundwater to deposit agate and chalcedony in fractures and porous zones. The Ute Mountain Ute people have inhabited this region for centuries, and the ridge has long been a landmark for travel and resource gathering across the Colorado Plateau.
tips: Search the base of the ridge and along shallow washes where water erosion has exposed weathered specimens - look for colorful banding in agate and the distinctive orange, red, and brown hues of desert jasper. Bring a small crack hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from exposed seams rather than hacking at the main ridge face. Early morning light makes spotting translucent agate easier; hold promising specimens up to the sun to see color and clarity before collecting.
terrain_notes: Elevation ranges from 5,500 to 6,200 feet. Terrain is open pinyon-juniper woodland with exposed sandstone outcrops and shallow sandy washes. Footing is generally stable but rocky and uneven in places. Walking distance from parking ranges from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on which section of ridge you access. Sparse vegetation provides minimal shade.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Summer heat (90-100F) is intense in this exposed terrain with minimal shade and limited water. Winter snow closes some access routes. Spring and fall offer moderate temperatures, clearer skies, and good visibility for spotting specimens.
written_directions: From Blanding, take US 191 south for approximately 8 miles. Turn east on Utah State Road 262 toward Monument Valley and drive about 15 miles. Look for informal pullouts and parking areas along the base of Comb Ridge on your right (east) side. GPS coordinates 37.303116, -109.656477 mark a popular collecting area. The ridge runs north-south and is highly visible as a dramatic topographic feature. Multiple access points exist along the ridge; choose based on your preferred hiking distance and target area.
road_conditions: Utah State Road 262 is paved. Access roads to parking areas vary from paved to rough dirt; passenger vehicles can reach main access points, though high-clearance vehicles are recommended for side roads.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along the ridge base. No developed parking lot; expect 2-6 vehicle capacity at individual spots. High-clearance vehicle recommended for some pullouts.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No power tools. Hand tools only (hammer, chisel, pry bar). Do not damage the main ridge formation or excavate large holes. Respect other collectors and leave areas in natural condition.

nearest_services: Blanding, approximately 8-15 miles west. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies available. Nearest water sources are in town.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and scattered low vegetation. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Slopes are gentle to moderate with no vertical climbing required. Walking distances of 0.5-2 miles from parking areas. Some washes involve minor rock scrambling. Sun exposure is intense - shade is minimal.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find at Comb Ridge in Utah?
faq.answer: Comb Ridge is known for beautiful agate specimens with colorful banding, desert jasper in orange and red hues, petrified wood fragments, and chalcedony. Agates here often display interesting color variation from iron oxides. Most specimens are found weathering out of sandstone layers along the ridge base and in shallow washes.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Comb Ridge?
faq.answer: No permit is required for rockhounding at Comb Ridge. It is located on public BLM land in Utah where recreational collecting is allowed under FLPMA guidelines. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use with no fees or advance registration needed.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound at Comb Ridge?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October are ideal. Summer temperatures exceed 90-100F with minimal shade and limited water. Winter snow can close access routes. Spring and fall offer moderate temperatures, clear skies, and excellent visibility for spotting agate and jasper specimens.

faq.question: How far is Comb Ridge from Blanding, and what are driving directions?
faq.answer: Comb Ridge is approximately 12 miles south of Blanding. Take US 191 south for 8 miles, then turn east on Utah State Road 262 toward Monument Valley and drive about 15 miles. Multiple informal parking pullouts line the ridge base along this route. The ridge is a dramatic visual landmark and easy to locate.

faq.question: Is Comb Ridge suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Comb Ridge is beginner-friendly and family-suitable. No special skills required - specimens weather naturally from exposed layers and are found by walking washes and the ridge base. Moderate hiking distances (0.5-2 miles) work for children age 8 and up. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and sturdy boots.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect agate and jasper at Comb Ridge?
faq.answer: A small crack hammer, chisel, and pry bar are ideal for carefully extracting specimens from exposed seams. Hand tools only are permitted - no power equipment. Many specimens can be picked up loose from washes without tools. A magnifying glass helps identify specimens in the field, and a UV light reveals fluorescence in some agates.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Comb Ridge near Blanding. Find agate, jasper, and petrified wood. No permit required. Open year-round.
